note_data.title: Functions
keyPoints: A **function** f is a relation where each element in the domain maps to exactly one element in the range
The **domain** is the complete set of possible input values (x-values) for which the function is defined
The **range** is the complete set of possible output values (y-values) that the function can produce
For composite function **fog(x) = f(g(x))**, substitute g(x) into function f, working from right to left
An **inverse function f⁻¹** exists only when f is one-to-one (each y-value corresponds to exactly one x-value)
To find f⁻¹(x): write y = f(x), rearrange to make x the subject, then interchange x and y
For a function to have an inverse, it must pass the **horizontal line test** (no horizontal line intersects the graph more than once)
The domain of f⁻¹ equals the range of f, and the range of f⁻¹ equals the domain of f
The graphs of f and f⁻¹ are **reflections of each other** in the line y = x

definitions.definition: A relation where each element in the domain maps to exactly one element in the codomain
The complete set of possible input values for which the function is defined
The complete set of actual output values that the function produces
A function formed by applying one function to the result of another, written as fog(x) = f(g(x))
A function f⁻¹ that undoes the operation of f, where f⁻¹(f(x)) = x and f(f⁻¹(x)) = x

howToAnswer.instruction: Give the domain or range as a set or inequality. No working needed.
Show all algebraic steps when finding composite or inverse functions
Demonstrate the result by substitution or algebraic manipulation

modelAnswer.question: The function f is defined by f(x) = 2x + 3 for x ∈ ℝ. The function g is defined by g(x) = x² - 1 for x ≥ 0. Find fg(x) and state its domain.

commonMistakes: Writing fg(x) = f(x) × g(x) instead of f(g(x)) - composite means substitution, not multiplication
When finding inverse functions, students forget to interchange x and y at the final step
Stating domain as 'all real numbers' when restrictions apply - always check for square roots, fractions, or given conditions
Confusing the order in composite functions - fog(x) means apply g first, then f
Not restricting the domain when finding inverse functions of many-to-one functions
examinerTips: Functions appear in every Paper 1, often combined with transformations or coordinate geometry
Always state the domain clearly using correct notation (inequalities or set notation)
When finding inverse functions, check your answer by verifying f(f⁻¹(x)) = x
For composite functions, work systematically from the inside out
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Graph showing function f and its inverse f⁻¹ as reflections in the line y = x, with clearly marked domain and range for both functions
